nameMeanings.name: Poitevin
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of French Poitevin: habitational name for someone from Poitou, a former province in western France whose capital city was Poitiers. See also Poitevent , compare Potvin 1.

variants.name: Potvin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Lucien, Armand, Gilles, Alphonse, Andre, Cecile, Michel, Pierre, Adelard, Adrien, Aime, Benoit.
French Canadian: altered form of French Poitevin (see Poitevint ). Compare Podvin 1 and Potwin .
In some cases possibly also French: variant (shortened form) of French Poitevin (see Poitevint , compare 1 above).

variants.name: Poitevent
meanings: History: This surname is listed along with its original form Poitevin in the (US) National Huguenot Society's register of qualified Huguenot ancestors. The Huguenot ancestor was Antoine Poitevin from France, who immigrated with his family to Charleston, SC, in 1685. His descendants also bear the surname Poitevint.

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .
